title: Natural and traditional remedies
summary: Natural does not mean proven or risk-free. Honey has the most useful symptom evidence; zinc may shorten a cold but certainty is low, while vitamin C, echinacea, elderberry, garlic and ginger have limited or inconsistent evidence.
evidenceSnapshot: Product formulation, timing and trial quality vary greatly. None of these remedies reliably cures influenza or replaces antiviral treatment for high-risk people.

interventions.name: Honey
interventions.finding: May ease acute cough and sleep in people over 12 months.
interventions.practical: Use a small spoonful or in a warm drink.
interventions.mechanism: Soothing coating and possible antimicrobial effects.
interventions.cautions: Never under 12 months; sugar and allergy considerations.

interventions.name: Zinc lozenges/oral zinc
interventions.finding: 2024 Cochrane review found possible average shortening by about two days, but evidence was low certainty and side effects were more common.
interventions.practical: If used, start early and follow a reputable product label; avoid prolonged high-dose use.
interventions.mechanism: May interfere with viral replication and local inflammatory signalling.
interventions.cautions: Nausea, bad taste and copper deficiency with prolonged excess; never use intranasal zinc.

interventions.name: Vitamin C
interventions.finding: Regular supplementation may slightly shorten colds in some people, but starting high doses after symptoms begin has not shown consistent benefit.
interventions.practical: Meet normal dietary requirements; avoid megadoses.
interventions.cautions: High doses can cause diarrhoea and kidney-stone risk in susceptible people.

interventions.name: Echinacea
interventions.finding: Results differ by species, plant part and extraction; no clear reliable preventive or treatment effect.
interventions.practical: Do not assume products are interchangeable.
interventions.cautions: Allergy risk, especially with daisy-family allergy; interactions possible.

interventions.name: Elderberry
interventions.finding: Small studies suggest possible symptom reduction, but evidence quality and independence are insufficient.
interventions.practical: Do not use raw or improperly prepared berries/leaves.
interventions.mechanism: Proposed antiviral and cytokine effects remain uncertain clinically.
interventions.cautions: Raw plant parts can cause toxicity; caution with immune conditions and pregnancy.

interventions.name: Garlic
interventions.finding: Insufficient evidence to treat an established cold; small prevention studies are not conclusive.
interventions.practical: Use as food rather than a high-dose cure.
interventions.mechanism: Laboratory antimicrobial effects do not establish human treatment benefit.
interventions.cautions: Supplements may increase bleeding risk and interact with medicines.

interventions.name: Ginger
interventions.finding: Can soothe nausea or provide a pleasant warm drink, but evidence for shortening colds is poor.
interventions.cautions: Concentrated products may affect bleeding or reflux.

interventions.name: Vitamin D
interventions.finding: Correcting deficiency supports health, but taking a large dose after symptoms start is not a proven cold treatment.
interventions.practical: Use routine supplementation only according to personal needs and Australian guidance.

practicalSteps: Ask what outcome matters: comfort, duration, prevention or severe-disease reduction.
Record supplements alongside medicines because interactions count.
Stop products causing rash, wheeze, vomiting or palpitations.
avoidOrCare: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, immune-suppressing treatment, anticoagulants and chronic disease warrant pharmacist/doctor review.
Do not give herbal products to infants without clinical advice.

myths.claim: If it is natural, it cannot interact with medicine.
myths.reality: Herbs and supplements can alter bleeding, sedation, blood pressure and drug metabolism.

myths.claim: Megadose vitamin C cures a cold.
myths.reality: Therapeutic megadoses after onset have not produced consistent clinical benefit.

myths.claim: Zinc always cuts two days off a cold.
myths.reality: That estimate is uncertain, trial results vary widely and side effects are common.

redFlags: Persistent vomiting or severe abdominal symptoms after a supplement
Suspected overdose or use of raw elderberry plant material
Using supplements instead of timely antiviral/medical care in a high-risk person
unsettled: Optimal zinc formulation, elemental dose, timing and lozenge dissolution pattern remain debated.
Elderberry evidence requires larger independent trials with standardised products.
